coredata.dc:title: In-situ growth of graphene quantum dots modified MoS2 membrane on tubular ceramic substrate with high permeability for both water and organic solvent
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Yue
Qin, Zhenping
Zhang, Xuehong
Wang, Naixin
Liu, Tongtong
Cui, Suping
An, Quan-Fu
Guo, Hongxia
coredata.dc:description: Molybdenum disulfide (MoS2) is widely used in membrane separation because of its outstanding chemical and structural stability. In this study, MoS2 nanosheets were in-situ grown on a tubular ceramic substrate through one-step hydrothermal synthesis with the graphene quantum dots (GQDs) incorporated in the precursor solution. The growth of the MoS2 nanosheets was inhibited due to the combination with GQDs through the Mo-O bond. Therefore, the number of transport channels in the MoS2/GQDs composite membrane were increased to improve the permeability. The results indicated that the MoS2/GQDs composite membrane have excellent permeances for both the aqueous solution and organic solvent. Moreover, the MoS2/GQDs composite membrane can be continuously operated in cross-flow filtration device for 100 h because the in-situ hydrothermal method has greatly improved the stability of membrane. The results indicated that the MoS2/GQDs composite membrane exhibits great potential in nanofiltration.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Molybdenum disulfide (MoS2)
Graphene quantum dots (GQDs)
Ceramic substrate
in-situ hydrothermal
Nanofiltration

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Structure and morphology of MoS2/GQDs composite membranes
Nanofiltration performance of the MoS2/GQDs composite membranes
Adsorption properties of MoS2/GQDs composite membranes
Stability of the MoS2/GQDs composite membranes
The comparison of the MoS2/GQDs composite membranes with reference

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the one-step in-situ hydrothermal synthesis of MoS2/GQDs composite membranes.
(a) UV–vis spectra and fluorescence luminescence, (b) FTIR spectra of the prepared GQDs, the (c) AFM and (d) TEM images of GQDs.

(a1) Lumen side and (b1) shell side surface morphology of ceramic tubular substrate. (a2) Lumen side and (b2) shell side surface morphology of MoS2/GQDs composite membrane. (c) The digital photo of MoS2/GQDs composite membrane.
Shell side surface morphology of (a) M/G-0, (b) M/G-1, (c) M/G-3, (d) M/G-4 membranes.
XRD patterns of MoS2 powder, substrate, M/G-0 and M/G-3 membrane.
(a) N2 adsorption/desorption isotherm and (b) pore volume change rate and cumulative pore volume of MoS2 and MoS2/GQDs powders.

EDS elemental mapping images of the surface of M/G-3 membrane. (a) Selected scanning area and (b–d) mapping images of Mo, S, and C element, (e) EDS element mapping analyses and (f) line scanning spectrograms of the cross-section of M/G-3.

(a and b) XPS survey spectra of M/G-0 and M/G-3 (c) high resolution scans of O element of M/G-0, (d) high resolution scans of C element of M/G-3, (e and f) high resolution scans of Mo element of M/G-0 and M/G-3 respectively.
Zeta potential of the membrane surface in different pH conditions.
Dynamic water contact angle of different composite membranes.
Nanofiltration performance of MoS2/GQDs composite membranes with different GQDs contents to (a) EB/H2O and (b) EB/MeOH solution.
Rejection of different dyes by M/G-3 membrane. (0.1 g/L dyes aqueous solution, cross-flow filtration at 0.2 MPa).
(a–c) UV–vis spectral changes of M/G-3 membrane for EB, RhB, and CV with different adsorption time, (d) adsorption of different dyes.

(a) Stability of M/G-3 membrane in water system (operating conditions: 0.2 MPa, 0.01 g/L EB/H2O solution) and (b) stability of M/G-2 membrane in MeOH system (operating conditions: 0.2 MPa, 0.01 g/L EB/MeOH solution).
Comparison of MoS2/GQDs composite membranes with the reported separation membranes. (a) Dye/water solution and (b) dye/MeOH solution.

head.ce:title: In-situ growth of graphene quantum dots modified MoS2 membrane on tubular ceramic substrate with high permeability for both water and organic solvent

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Materials and Manufacturing, Beijing University of Technology, Key Laboratory of Advanced Functional Materials, Ministry of Education, Beijing, 100124, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Advanced Functional Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Faculty of Materials and Manufacturing, Beijing University of Technology, Key Laboratory of Advanced Functional Materials, Ministry of Education, Beijing 100124, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Beijing Key Laboratory for Green Catalysis and Separation, Department of Environmental and Chemical Engineering, Beijing University of Technology, Beijing, 100124, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Beijing Key Laboratory for Green Catalysis and Separation
Department of Environmental and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Beijing Key Laboratory for Green Catalysis and Separation, Department of Environmental and Chemical Engineering, Beijing University of Technology, Beijing 100124, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Molybdenum disulfide (MoS2) is widely used in membrane separation because of its outstanding chemical and structural stability. In this study, MoS2 nanosheets were in-situ grown on a tubular ceramic substrate through one-step hydrothermal synthesis with the graphene quantum dots (GQDs) incorporated in the precursor solution. The growth of the MoS2 nanosheets was inhibited due to the combination with GQDs through the Mo-O bond. Therefore, the number of transport channels in the MoS2/GQDs composite membrane were increased to improve the permeability. The results indicated that the MoS2/GQDs composite membrane have excellent permeances for both the aqueous solution and organic solvent. Moreover, the MoS2/GQDs composite membrane can be continuously operated in cross-flow filtration device for 100 h because the in-situ hydrothermal method has greatly improved the stability of membrane. The results indicated that the MoS2/GQDs composite membrane exhibits great potential in nanofiltration.

ce:section.ce:para: With the development of the modern society, numerous industrial products (organic dyes, oil, heavy metal ions, and organic solvent) are discharged into the environment, which causes serious pollution. It is particularly important to develop green and efficient separation technology. As a promising membrane separation technology, nanofiltration (NF) is widely used in separating solutes from water and organic solvent [1–4]. The separation performance of the NF membrane, such as the selectivity, permeability, and stability are mainly determined by membrane material and structure. Therefore, the development of novel NF membrane material and regulation of membrane structure are crucial.

Two-dimensional layered materials, such as graphene oxide (GO) [5], metal-organic framework (MOFs) [6], MXene [7], and molybdenum disulfide (MoS2) [8,9] have been considered as ideal materials for constructing NF membrane with high separation performance due to their atomic-level thickness and controllable nanochannel. Among them, MoS2 has been considered an ideal material to prepare the separation membrane, due to its advantages of good mechanical property, antifouling property, thermal and chemical stability. Peng et al. [10] assembled a laminar membrane with atom-thick MoS2 nanosheets via vacuum filtration. The obtained membranes had the same rejection with the GO membrane, while the water permeances increased by 3–5 times. Wang et al. [11] prepared irregular MoS2 nanosheets with different thickness via a temperate ultrasonic-assisted exfoliation method. A loosely stacked lamellar membrane was obtained through the slow vacuum filtration. The permeability of the membrane to water and organic was greatly improved. To further improve the separation performance of the MoS2 membrane, the structure and property of the MoS2 nanosheets and their composite membranes should be modified, such as the size of the nanosheets and the number of transport channels in the membrane. Doping other components is an effective modification method to control the structure of MoS2 membrane.

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are new carbon-based materials with zero dimension, which is composed of small fragments of a single-layer or multi-layer graphene (size less than 100 nm) [12]. GQDs have attracted tremendous attention due to its abundant oxygen-containing functional groups (-OH, -COOH), large specific surface area and unique quantum confinement effect [13,14]. Recently, it has been shown that the growth of MoS2 crystal can be affected and manipulated by GQDs. Zhang et al. [15] doped GQDs into MoS2 nanosheets via a solvothermal method. MoS2 was transformed from pristine agglomerated particles into scattered and thinner appearance. Therefore, the interlayer distance of the MoS2 nanosheets was expanded by GQDs to improve the lithium storage performance. Sangpour et al. [16] prepared GQDs/MoS2 composite by a one-step hydrothermal method. The addition of GQDs changed the growth orientation of MoS2 nanosheets from parallel to vertical. This structure provided a larger surface area and porosity, which facilitate ion diffusion. To our best knowledge, most MoS2/GQDs composite materials are mainly utilized in the field of electrocatalyst and lithium-ion batteries. Their application on membrane separation has not been explored.

In this study, we report a new strategy of preparing MoS2/GQDs nanofiltration membrane by in-situ hydrothermal method. GQDs were used as additive to regulate the structure of MoS2 nanosheets on membrane. MoS2 nanosheets with small size were obtained to form more transport channels in the membrane. The obtained MoS2/GQDs composite membrane could be used both in aqueous solution and in an organic solvent for separating different dye molecules.

42.0 mm-long Al2O3 tubular substrate with 9.0 mm inner and 13.0 mm outer diameters were supplied by JieXi LiShun Technology Co., Ltd. (Guangdong, China). The average pore size of the Al2O3 tubular substrate was about 50.0 nm. Ammonium molybdate tetrahydrate ((NH4)6Mo7O24·4H2O) and thiourea (CH4N2S) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Co. (USA). Dyes such as Evans blue (EB, 960.8 g/mol), eriochrome black T (EBT, 461.4 g/mol), rhodamine B (RhB, 479.0 g/mol), crystal violet (CV, 408.0 g/mol), methylene blue (MeB, 319.9 g/mol), xylenol orange (XO, 716.6 g/mol) and rose bengal (RB, 1017.6 g/mol) were supplied by Beijing Chemical Factory (Beijing, China). Citric acid was purchased from Beijing Inokai Technology Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). Methanol (MeOH) was purchased from Beijing Chemical Factory (Beijing, China). Dialysis bag with MWCO of 1000 Da was purchased from Nanjing SenBeiJia Biological Technology Co., Ltd. (Nanjing, China). All the reagents were of analytical grade and used as received without further purification. Deionized water was used throughout this study.

In this study, GQDs was prepared by direct pyrolysis of citric acid according to the “bottom-up” approach [12,17]. Specifically, 10 g citric acid was added to the round bottom flask and heated to 190 °C. After heating for 10 min, the liquid was changed from colorless to orange. Then, the liquid was poured into 50 mL 1.0 M NaOH solution immediately under vigorous magnetic stirring. Subsequently, the solution was further neutralized to pH = 7 by HCl, followed by dialyzed with MWCO of 1000 Da dialysis bag for 24 h. Finally, the solution in the bag was dried in a vacuum oven to obtain GQDs powder with light yellow color.

As shown in Fig. 1 , MoS2/GQDs composite membranes were prepared by the in-situ hydrothermal method. 2.2 mM ammonium molybdate tetrahydrate ((NH4)6Mo7O24·4H2O), 72 mM thiourea (CH4N2S) and a certain amount of GQDs from 0 to 9.1 wt% were added into 18 mL aqueous solution with under fully stirring to form a precursor solution of MoS2. Then, the milk white tubular ceramic substrates were completely immersed in the precursor solution. The pores in the substrate were filled with the precursor solution by vacuum suction method, followed by transformed into a Teflon autoclave and carried out the hydrothermal reaction at 220 °C for 30 h. Subsequently, the obtained membranes with black color were taken out after the autoclave cooled down to room temperature. In the end, the MoS2/GQDs composite membranes were cleaned by deionized water and ethanol and then dried at 60 °C for 12 h. The membranes prepared with 0, 3.2, 4.5, 6.3, and 9.1 wt% of GQDs contents were named as M/G-0, M/G-1, M/G-2, M/G-3, and M/G-4, respectively. In order to facilitate the analysis of the binding mode between GQDs and MoS2, the pure MoS2 powder and MoS2/GQDs composite powder with 6.3 wt% of GQDs were prepared according to the same preparation conditions as above.

The X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were obtained using D8 advance diffractometer (Bruker/AXS, Germany) with Cu Ka radiation (1.5406 Å) under 40 kV and 30 mA in the range of 10°–80° with a scan step of 0.02°. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were performed on an Escalab 250 Xi (Thermo Fisher, USA) with a monochromatic Al Kα X-ray source. The Fourier transform infrared spectra (FTIR) were carried out on a Vertex 70 FTIR spectrometer (Bruker, Germany) with a scan range of 4000-500 cm−1. The UV absorption spectrum and absorbance at maximum absorption wavelength were characterized by a Lambda 750 UV/Vis/NIR spectrophotometer (PerkinElmer, USA) with a scan range of 250–800 nm. The thickness of GQDs was observed by atomic force microscopy (AFM) (Dimension Icon, Bruker, USA) and the size of GQDs was observed by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) on a JEM-2010 microscope (JEOL, Ltd., Japan). The morphologies of the MoS2/GQDs composite membranes were observed by SU8020 scanning electron microscopy (SEM) (Hitachi, Japan). The elemental compositions of the membranes were analyzed using an energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) device attached to SEM. Zeta potentials of the membrane surfaces were measured on a Zetasizer Nano ZS90 (Malvern, UK) in water at 25 °C. The pH value was adjusted to 3, 5, 7, 9 and 11 with 0.1 M HCl and NaOH solution. The tubular ceramic membrane was cut and polished to the right size (3 × 4 × 1 mm), then fixed on the sample cell. The wettability of the membranes was evaluated using dynamic contact angle analysis on a DSA100 Contact Angle Goniometer (Kruss, Germany). The specific surface areas, pore volumes and average pore sizes of MoS2 and MoS2/GQDs powders were obtained by V-Sorb 2800 TP Surface Area and porosimetry Analyzer (GAPP Instruments, China).

The performance of the fabricated composite membranes was investigated by a home-made cross-flow filtration device with an effective membrane area of 9.6 cm2. The dyes concentration of the feed solution was 0.1 or 0.01 g/L, which were circulated by a pump under the pressure of 0.2 MPa. The feed solution was driven by pressure through the shell side of the membrane and the permeate was collected from the lumen side of the tubular membrane at time intervals of 0.5 h. The concentration of dyes in feed and permeate were analyzed by a UV–vis spectrophotometer at the maximal absorption wavelength of the dyes. The performance of the membranes characterized by permeance (P) and rejection (R) of dye solute was calculated respectively by equations as follows:
(1)
P =
V
A t · Δ P
where V is the permeate solution volume, A is the effective filtration area, t is the recording time, and P is the transmembrane pressure.
(2)
R =
(
1 −
C p
C f
)
× 100 %
where C
p and C
f are the concentrations of the dyes in permeate and feed, respectively.

The adsorption of dye on the membrane was evaluated by immersing the prepared composite membrane in 30 mL of dye solutions with concentration of 0.01 g/L for 72 h until adsorption equilibrium. The concentrations of the dye solutions were analyzed through a UV–vis spectrophotometer at the maximal absorption wavelength of the dye. The adsorption (A) of dyes were calculated by equations:
(3)
A =
(
1 −
C 0
C t
)
× 100 %
where C
0 and C
t are the initial and final dye concentrations, respectively.

The GQDs were prepared by the pyrolysis of citric acid (CA) at 190 °C. The fluorescence, UV, FTIR and AFM characterizations were carried out to demonstrate the transformation of citric acid into GQDs. As shown in Fig. 2 a, citric acid had no absorption peak under the UV irradiation of 280–600 nm, while the GQDs showed strong UV absorption at 360 nm due to the n-π* bond transition of C=O. From the inset of Fig. 2a, the GQDs solution emitted blue light when excited with a 365 nm UV beam (left), which present unique optical and electronic properties due to their quantum confinement and edge effects [18]. In contrast, the citric acid solution exhibited colorless (right). The FTIR spectra in Fig. 2b indicated that both citric acid and GQDs showed a broad and strong peak at 3350 cm-1 and an obvious peak at 1635 cm-1, which was ascribed to the stretching vibrations of hydroxyl (-OH) and carboxyl (-COOH) groups, respectively [17]. Besides, the FTIR spectra of GQDs indicated two new tiny peaks at 1560 and 1380 cm-1, which belong to the C=C stretching vibration and C-H stretching vibration of aromatic ring, respectively [19]. These results indicated the GQDs were prepared with the structure of aromatic rings bearing both -OH and -COOH groups. The morphology of the GQDs was also observed by AFM and TEM. As shown in Fig. 2(c and d), the GQDs were mostly nanosheets of 30–40 nm in size with a thickness of about 1.5 nm, which indicated that most of the GQDs were 4~5 layers [20].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structure and morphology of MoS2/GQDs composite membranes
ce:section.ce:para: During the preparation of the composite membranes, the ceramic tubular substrates were completely immersed in the precursor solution. MoS2/GQDs might grow on both the shell side and lumen side of the substrates. Therefore, the distinction between shell side and lumen side of the MoS2/GQDs composite membranes were studied. The SEM images of lumen sides and shell sides of ceramic tube and MoS2/GQDs composite membrane were shown in Fig. 3 . It can be seen from Fig. 3(a1 and b1) that the shell side of ceramic tube substrate had a more compact and uniform surface than the lumen side. The average pore size of the shell side is 50 nm, and the lumen side has a wide pore size distribution (0.2~1 μm). In addition, the MoS2/GQDs layer on the shell side was uniform and dense, but only dispersed MoS2/GQDs was observed in the lumen side (Fig. 3a2 and b2). Moreover, it can be seen from Fig. 3c that most of MoS2/GQDs grew on the shell side of substrate. The reason for this result was that the lumen side with large pore size was not conducive to form a continuous and uniform separation layer. Moreover, the macroscopic shape of the interface is an important factor for crystal during growth process. The convex interface tends to produce fewer defects and favorable grain selection conditions [21].

The effect of GQDs amount on the morphology of shell side separation layer was further studied. As shown in Fig. 4 a1, without GQDs modification, MoS2 grew uniformly on the shell side of substrate in the shape of nanoflowers. However, it can be clearly observed from Fig. 4a2 that the nanoflowers were formed by the agglomeration of MoS2 nanosheets. When the content of GQDs was 3.2%, the agglomeration of MoS2 nanosheets was intensified on the surface of the membrane (Fig. 4b1). As shown in Fig. 4b2, the size of MoS2 nanosheets decreased with the addition of GQDs. This is due to the GQDs limited the growth of MoS2 nanosheets [22]. In the preparation of pristine MoS2, two major steps are involved: the initial nucleation of amorphous primary MoS2 nanoparticles and the following assembly process [15]. After GQDs was added into the precursor solution, due to the chelating connection between [Mo7O24]6- and GQDs, [Mo7O24]6- embedded layered-GQDs structure was assembled, which inhibited the growth of layered MoS2 crystals, especially in the (002) plane of MoS2 [22,23]. More transport channels may be formed due to the smaller MoS2 nanosheets, which would improve the permeability of the membrane. When the content of GQDs was 6.3 wt% (Fig. 4c1), the MoS2 nanosheets had agglomerated into a spherical shape, the size of MoS2 nanosheets further decreased with the number of nanosheets increased (Fig. 4c2). However, when the content of GQDs was 9.1 wt%, the size and number of nanosheets did not change significantly (Fig. 4d2). In addition, the defects were found on the surface of the membranes, which might reduce the rejection (Fig. 4d1).

The influence of GQDs on the crystallization of MoS2 was further explored by XRD. As shown in Fig. 5 , the characteristic peaks of MoS2 and substrate are detected in M/G-0 and M/G-3 membrane, which indicated that the MoS2 was successfully grown on the substrate. In addition, no peak shifting was found on M/G-1 and M/G-3, which indicated that the incorporation of GQDs had little influence on the interlayer spacing of the MoS2. Nevertheless, the intensity of the 002 peak in M/G-3 was obviously weakened and the width was increased, which indicated that the poor crystallinity of MoS2. This result further verified the inhibitory effect of GQDs on MoS2 growth.

To further explore the effect of the MoS2 nanosheet size on the transport channels, MoS2 and MoS2/GQDs powders were characterized by BET. As shown in Fig. 6 a, all the obtained N2 adsorption and desorption isotherms belong to the IV type and the hysteresis loop belong to the H3 type, which indicated the presence of mesopores in the MoS2 and MoS2/GQDs [24]. By Brunauer-Emmett-Teller equation calculation, the specific surface area of MoS2 and MoS2/GQDs were 31.2 m2/g and 44.4 m2/g, respectively. The increase of specific surface area can be attributed to the decrease of the size and the increase of the number of MoS2 nanosheets. Fig. 6b showed that the MoS2/GQDs powder had a larger pore volume (0.20 m2/g) and smaller average pore size (13.3 nm) than MoS2 powder, which were 0.15 m2/g and 15.2 nm, respectively. Since the pore volume was the sum of volumes for single pore, the increased of pore volume and the decreased of average pore size indicated that the number of pores were increased.

EDS elemental analyses was used to study the structure and element composition of the MoS2/GQDs composite membrane. As shown in Fig. 7 (a–d), Mo, S, and C elements could be found on the surface of M/G-3. Moreover, the shape of the Mo, S and C elements were consistent with the MoS2 nanoflowers. This confirmed that GQDs were successfully doped and uniformly distributed in MoS2 nanosheets. From the cross-sectional EDS element analyses of M/G-3 (Fig. 7e), it could be seen that Mo, S, and C elements were mainly concentrated on the surface of the ceramic substrate, forming a separation layer with a thickness of about 3 μm. The Al element from the ceramic substrate could be detected under the separation layer. Moreover, a small amount of Mo and S elements were distributed inside the ceramic substrate, which had less influence on the separation performance of the membrane (Fig. 7f).

XPS was carried out in order to further investigate the chemical composition of M/G-0 and M/G-3. As shown in Fig. 8 a and b, the M/G-0 and M/G-3 mainly contained Mo, S, C, O elements. Since the XPS test depth was less than 10 nm, which was far less than the thickness of separation layer, the Al element in ceramic substrate could not be detected. The C element detected in the M/G-0 could be attributed to the unreacted thiourea in the precursor solution. The two peaks at 533.3 and 531.2 eV could be seen from the high-resolution XPS spectra of O element of M/G-3 (Fig. 8c). The deconvolution of the O1s spectrum indicates two contributions of lattice oxygen O2− (in the stronger Mo-O bond) at the lower binding energy of 531.2 eV and surface adsorbed oxygen (-OH group and chemisorbed oxygen-containing species) at higher bonding energy of 533.1 eV [25]. The high-resolution XPS spectra of C element of M/G-3 shown in Fig. 8d had three new binding energies at 284.1, 285.2 and 288.4 eV, which correspond to the C-C bonds, C-O bonds and C=O bonds, respectively [26]. The appearance of C-O bonds and C=O bonds could be attributed to the GQDs, which proved the existence of GQDs in the composite membrane. The high-resolution XPS spectra of Mo element of M/G-0 shown in Fig. 8e suggested that the binding energies of Mo 3d3/2 and Mo 3d5/2 of Mo4 + peaks located at 232.3 and 229.1 eV confirmed the chemical composition of MoS2 [27]. However, the high-resolution XPS spectra of Mo element of M/G-3 shown in Fig. 8f had a dramatic difference. Here was a new oxidation state at 235.6 eV, which was related to the Mo 3d5/2 of Mo6 + (typical of the Mo-O band) [8,28–30]. Therefore, it is concluded that MoS2 and GQDs were connected by Mo-O bond.

The surface charge has a significant influence on the performance of the membrane. Therefore, the surface charge of MoS2/GQDs composite membranes were studied with the change of GQDs contents. The Zeta potential of the membrane surface in different pH conditions were shown in Fig. 9 . The results exhibited that the isoelectric point (IEP) of the MoS2/GQDs composite membrane was about 3. All the MoS2/GQDs composite membranes had a negatively charged surface when the pH was higher than the isoelectric point of membrane. When the pH was ~7, the surface of MoS2/GQDs composite membrane was negative. Notably, with the increase of GQDs content, the surface charge of the composite membrane increased. Wang et al. [31] found that the charge was accumulated on the sites of S atoms by constructing charge-dipole model and using density functional theory calculations. Therefore, the charge property of MoS2 was mainly contributed by S atoms, while the GQDs replaced some S atoms and combined with Mo atoms, leading to the decrease of negative charge of the MoS2. Then, the charge effect of the membranes would be weakened to influence the separation performance of the membrane.

In order to explore the influence of GQDs on the permeability of the membrane, the dynamic water contact angle of the membranes was tested. As shown in Fig. 10 , with the increase of GQDs content, the permeation time of water decreased obviously. The results showed that the modification of GQDs could improve the permeability of the membrane. This phenomenon might be attributed to the incorporation of GQDs inhibited the growth of MoS2. More transport channels were obtained to increase the mass transfer of water molecules.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Nanofiltration performance of the MoS2/GQDs composite membranes
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, the nanofiltration performances of composite membranes with different GQDs contents were studied for separating dye molecules from water. 0.01 g/L Evans blue (EB) aqueous solution was used as a feed with cross-flow filtration at 0.2 MPa. The experimental results are shown in Fig. 11 a, the water permeance of the ceramic substrate was 998 L/m2·h·MPa with the rejection for EB was only 75.7%. The water permeance of the membrane without GQDs (M/G-0) was 190 L/m2·h·MPa with the rejection for EB was 98.5%. When the 3.2 wt% GQDs were added, the water permeance increased obviously, which was 298 L/m2·h·MPa with the rejection for EB remained at 98.7%. To optimize the separation performance, 6.3 and 9.1 wt% of GQDs were added to prepare the composite membranes. When the amount of GQDs was 6.3 wt%, the M/G-3 membrane had excellent water permeance (L/m2·h·MPa) and the rejection for EB was kept at 98.1%. However, when the content of GQDs increased to 9.1 wt%, the water permeance of the M/G-4 membrane wasn't significantly improved, while the rejection for EB was only 95.1%. The change of membrane properties could be explained from the structure and surface properties of the membrane. Due to the limited growth of MoS2 by GQDs, the size of MoS2 nanosheets decreased and more transport channels were formed, thus improving the permeability of the membrane. However, with the increase of GQDs content, the negatively charge of the membrane decreased (Fig. 9b), and the charge effect between the membrane and dye weakens, resulting in a decrease in the separation performance, which was consistent with the Donnan effect theory. Moreover, excessive GQDs could lead to defects on the surface of the membrane (Fig. 4d1), resulting in a decrease in separation performance.

Since the MoS2/GQDs composite membrane was formed on the surface of the ceramic tube, the composite membrane may have excellent solvent resistance. Therefore, it can also be used in organic solvent nanofiltration [8,32]. Therefore, the nanofiltration performance of composite membranes with different GQDs content was evaluated for separating dye from MeOH. 0.01 g/L EB/MeOH solution was used as a feed with cross-flow filtration at 0.2 MPa. As shown in Fig. 11b, the MeOH permeance of the ceramic substrate was 7187 L/m2·h·MPa with the rejection for EB was only 5.1%. The MeOH permeance of the membrane without GQDs (M/G-0) were 202 L/m2·h·MPa with the rejection for EB was 97.2%. When the 3.2 wt% GQDs were added, the MeOH permeance of M/G-1 was 525 L/m2·h·MPa and the rejection for EB remained at 94.7%. When the 4.5 wt% GQDs were added, the MeOH permeance of M/G-2 was 780 L/m2·h·MPa and the rejection for EB remained at 92.4%. However, the rejection for EB in the composite membrane prepared by adding 9.1 wt% GQDs (M/G-4) was only 78%. Therefore, the optimum amount of GQDs needed was smaller than that used in aqueous solution. This can be attributed to the different viscosity and dielectric constant of methanol and water. Methanol (0.55 mPa·s at 25 °C) had lower viscosity than water (0.89 mPa·s at 25 °C), so the frictional force between liquid and channel was smaller and easier to penetrate. Therefore, the permeability of the composite membrane prepared under the same conditions to methanol was higher than that to water [33]. In addition, the rejection of EB in the methanol was lower than that in the water. This can be attributed to the lower dielectric constant of methanol than water and the weak Donnan effect in the separation process [34]. Therefore, the prepared MoS2/GQDs composite membranes had excellent nanofiltration performance not only in aqueous solution (water permeance was 645 L/m2·h·MPa, EB rejection was 98%), but also in methanol solution (MeOH permeance was 780 L/m2·h·MPa, EB rejection was more than 92%).

The rejection of the nanofiltration membrane was affected by both the Donnan effect and pore size sieving [35,36]. In order to explore the main factors affecting the rejection, the rejection of different charged dyes in the water system was explored by M/G-3 membranes. Six different dyes (Table S1, ESI†), such as chrome black T (EBT), rose bengal (RB), xylenol orange (XO), methylene blue (MeB), crystal violet (CV), and rhodamine B (RhB) were selected to evaluate the membrane performance. The rejection of the membrane was studied under the same conditions for dyes with different charges (Feed was 0.1 g/L dyes aqueous solution, cross-flow filtration at 0.2 MPa). As shown in Fig. 12 , the rejections for negatively charged dyes (EB, EBT, RB, XO) of MoS2/GQDs composite membrane were higher than 90%. However, the rejections of positively charged dyes (MeB, CV) and charged neutral dye (RhB) were less than 10%. Although GQDs reduced the surface charge of the MoS2 membrane, it still showed a negative charge (Fig. 9b). Therefore, it was concluded that the rejection of the MoS2/GQDs composite membranes was mainly influenced by the Donnan effect.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Adsorption properties of MoS2/GQDs composite membranes
ce:section.ce:para: At present, some membranes rely on the adsorption of materials to achieve the purpose of separation [37,38]. To investigate the adsorption effect of MoS2/GQDs composite membranes on the separation performance, the adsorption experiments were carried out by using positively charged, negatively charged, and charged neutral dyes. As shown in Fig. 13 , the adsorption capacity of M/G-3 membranes for EB, RhB and CV was 8.3%, 18.3% and 47.0%, respectively. The result showed that the M/G-3 membrane was easier to absorb positively charged dyes (CV), due to the electrostatic interaction. However, the adsorption capacity for negatively charged dye (EB) and neutral charged dye (RhB) were low. It is indicated that the rejection of dye by MoS2/GQDs composite membrane was not due to the adsorption.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stability of the MoS2/GQDs composite membranes
ce:section.ce:para: For practical application, the long-term running stability of the membrane is important. The M/G-2 and M/G-3 membranes were used for separating 0.01 g/L EB/H2O solution and EB/MeOH solution at 0.2 MPa for 100 h, respectively. As shown in Fig. 14 a, the rejection for EB of the M/G-3 membrane was above 97%, while the water permeance could maintain above 600 L/m2·h·MPa. Similarly, the rejection for EB of the M/G-2 membrane was above 90%, while the MeOH permeance could maintain above 600 L/m2·h·MPa (Fig. 14b). The excellent long-term operation stability of the MoS2/GQDs composite membranes was attributed to the rigid structure and good chemical stability of MoS2 [39]. More importantly, the MoS2/GQDs were in-situ grown on the surface of a tubular ceramic substrate, which provided strong binding force between the separation layer and substrate. Therefore, the MoS2/GQDs composite membrane has a good stability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The comparison of the MoS2/GQDs composite membranes with reference
ce:section.ce:para: The separation performance of MoS2/GQDs composite membrane was compared with the membranes in the literature (Table S2, ESI†). The membranes tested using dye/water and dye/MeOH solution were selected for comparison. As shown in Fig. 15 , the obtained MoS2/GQDs composite membrane showed notable high solvent permeances and reasonable solute rejections compared with other membranes both in water and methanol solvent. Moreover, MoS2/GQDs composites membranes have good structural stability and solvent resistance.

In general, the MoS2/GQDs composite membranes were prepared by in-situ hydrothermal synthesis. The growth of MoS2 was inhibited through incorporation of GQDs in the precursor solution. Therefore, MoS2 nanosheets with small size were obtained to form more transport channels. The permeability of the membrane was thus improved. The MoS2/GQDs composite membranes had excellent nanofiltration performance not only in aqueous solution (water permeance was 645 L/m2·h·MPa, EB rejection was 98%), but also in methanol solution (MeOH permeance was 780 L/m2·h·MPa, EB rejection was more than 92%). In addition, the MoS2/GQDs composite membranes could be continuously operated in cross-flow filtration device for 100 h in water and methanol solution without no obvious performance degradation. This study would provide a new strategy for the preparation and modification of MoS2 nanofiltration membrane.

Yue Liu: Investigation, Data curation, Formal analysis, Validation, Writing – original draft. Zhenping Qin: Conceptualization, Methodology. Xuehong Zhang: Data curation, Formal analysis. Naixin Wang: Methodology, Data curation, Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition. Tongtong Liu: Formal analysis. Suping Cui: Formal analysis, Funding acquisition. Quan-Fu An: Formal analysis. Hongxia Guo: Conceptualization, Methodology, Data curation, Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (21878003, 21476005), the Importation and Development of High-Caliber Talents Project of Beijing Municipal Institutions (CIT&TCD201904014), and the National Natural Science Fund for Innovative Research Groups (No. 51621003).
